allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and cooking wine)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of braised pork intestine with Laba garlic is approximately 250 g, served as a shared main dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, large intestine, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 10397) adapted with braising sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine (NDB 10397 / FDC ID 169366). Values adjusted upward for sodium and fat to account for braising sauce (soy sauce, dark soy sauce, cooking wine, sugar). Carbohydrate and sugar values reflect added Laba garlic and sugar in the braising liquid. This is an estimated adaptation; no exact HPB or ICMR entry exists for this specific Beijing dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pork intestine with Laba garlic. GI estimated at 32 based on the analogous profile of a high-protein, moderate-fat braised meat dish with low net carbohydrate content. Reference anchor: braised pork belly / mixed braised meat dishes with soy-based sauce, GI approximately 28–40 (low range), per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Laba garlic (vinegar-pickled) contributes minimal glycemic carbohydrates. GL per 250 g serving = (32 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1300 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but moderate fat and cholesterol load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index (estimated 32) and low net carbohydrate content make this dish relatively glycemia-friendly for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat and cholesterol content warrant portion control and infrequent consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium from pork intestine and braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork large intestine is inherently high in cholesterol (approximately 135 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (approximately 4.6 g per 100 g), and the soy-based braising sauce adds substantial sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Intestine with Laba Garlic (腊八蒜烧肥肠, Là Bā Suàn Shāo Féi Cháng) is a traditional Beijing Chinese dish that pairs slow-braised pork large intestine with Laba garlic — a distinctive condiment made by pickling garlic cloves in rice vinegar during the Laba Festival period (the eighth day of the twelfth lunar month), which turns the garlic emerald green and imparts a mellow, tangy, slightly pungent flavour. The pork intestine is first thoroughly cleaned, blanched to remove impurities, and then braised in a rich sauce of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, scallions, star anise, and sugar until tender and deeply flavoured. The Laba garlic is added toward the end of cooking or served alongside, contributing acidity and aromatic complexity that cuts through the richness of the offal. The texture of the finished dish is soft, gelatinous, and yielding, with a savory-sweet-sour flavour profile. Nutritionally, pork large intestine is a moderate source of protein (approximately 13.5 g per 100 g) and is relatively high in fat (approximately 12.8 g per 100 g), saturated fat (approximately 4.6 g per 100 g), and cholesterol (approximately 135 mg per 100 g). The dish is high in sodium due to the soy-based braising liquid (approximately 520 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 32) owing to the minimal net carbohydrate content. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and cholesterol load. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. In Singapore, this dish is served at Chinese restaurants specialising in Beijing or northern Chinese cuisine.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine data with estimated adjustments for the braising sauce. GI is an analogous estimate based on the international GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2021). Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cooked pork large intestine) is well-characterised in USDA data and the adaptations are conservative and documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169366, cooked pork large intestine) with upward sodium adjustment (~+180 mg/100g) and minor carbohydrate/sugar adjustment to account for soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, and Laba garlic in the braising liquid. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food item. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food item. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (32, low) based on Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables using braised meat with soy-based sauce as the closest analogue; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. GL per 250 g serving = (32 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.2. 9. This dish is not halal (pork) and not kosher (pork). 10. The restaurant source (See Ya Tonight 今晚有局, Singapore) is noted for provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
